coredata.dc:title: Tailored polyethyleneimine-based fluorescent nanoparticles for functionalized applications in detection and adsorption of cobalt (II)
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Jin
Chen, Weixing
Ma, Yiting
Bright, Geoffrey
Jie, Xile
Zhao, Huaqi
Zhou, Hongwei
Jin, Xilang
coredata.dc:description: Facile fabrication of water-soluble fluorescent polymer nanoprobes for trace detection of cobalt ion (Co2+) is still a demand in environmental and biological fields. The sphere-like nanoscale PEI-MA was successfully synthesized by hyperbranched polyethyleneimine (hPEI) and mercaptosuccinic acid (MA) via hydrothermal treatment. The synthesized PEI-MA had good selectivity and sensitivity, with a wide detection range (0–200 μM) and a low detection limit (0.27 μM) to Co2+ in aqueous solution. The low bio-toxicity and cell permeability were demonstrated by HeLa cell imaging, indicating the PEI-MA with certain potential could be utilized in the field of biosensing domain. Meanwhile, the PEI-MA was employed in adjustable fluorescent dyes and fabrication of portable solid sensors. Among these PEI-DA-doped sensors, test strips and nanofibrous fluorescent films with remarkable fluorescent performance could be used as effective monitoring devices for the detection of Co2+. Moreover, the dual-functional fluorescent hydrogels owned functions of detection and adsorption for Co2+ in water system, providing ingenious reference for the interdisciplinary research between fluorescent sensing and adsorption of Co2+.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Co2+
Fluorescence nanoparticles
Cell imaging
Solid sensors
Adsorption hydrogels

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and the size distribution of PEI-MA. (b) XRD pattern of PEI-MA. (c) FT-IR spectra of hPEI and PEI-MA. (d) 1H NMR spectrum of PEI-MA. (e–i) XPS spectra and high-resolution XPS spectra of PEI-MA.

(a) Absorption spectra of hPEI, MA and PEI-MA. (b) Excitation and emission spectra of PEI-MA. (c) Fluorescence emission of PEI-MA under different excitation wavelengths (340–390 nm). (d) CIE chromaticity coordinates of PEI-MA. (e) Fluorescence titration curve of PEI-MA and Co2+ (0–200 μM). (f) Linear relationship between PEI-MA and Co2+ (fluorescence intensity at 474 nm). (g) Ion competition experiment of PEI-MA. Pillars in the front row: PEI-MA + Co2+ (200 μM) + other 19 cation analytes (1 mM). Pillars in the back row: PEI-MA + other 19 cation analytes (1 mM). (h) Fluorescence intensity of PEI-MA and PEI-DA-Co2+ in different pH conditions. (i) Fluorescence intensity of PEI-MA and PEI-MA-Co2+ at saline solution. λex = 350 nm.

(a) Absorption spectra of PEI-MA, Co2+ and PEI-MA + Co2+. (b) FT-IR spectra of PEI-MA and PEI-MA + Co2+. (c–g) XPS spectra and high-resolution XPS spectra of PEI-MA + Co2+. (h) Zeta potential distribution of PEI-MA and PEI-MA + Co2+. (i) Fluorescence lifetimes of PEI-MA, PEI-MA + Co2+.

(a) Fluorescent images of HeLa cells were cultivated with PEI-MA solution (10 μg/mL) for 30 min at 37 °C (scale bar = 75 μm). (b) Fluorescent images of HeLa cells pre-incubated with PEI-MA and then incubated with Co2+(60 μM). (λex: 350 nm, λem: 430–530 nm).
Photographs under UV light (365 nm): (a) Fluorescent dyes. (b) Test strips. (c) PLA nanofibrous fluorescence films. (d) SEM image of nanofibrous films.

Photographs under UV light (365 nm): (a) Illustration of PEI-MA-doped difunctional hydrogel. (b) Detection for Co2+. (c) PEI-MA-doped hydrogel under different concentrations of Co2+. (d) SEM image of solvent-free sensing hydrogel. (e) Adsorption equilibrium data of sensing hydrogel. (f) Fitting curves of adsorption kinetics of sensing hydrogel.
Illustration of PEI-MA synthesis and the detection of Co2+ in aqueous solution.

head.ce:title: Tailored polyethyleneimine-based fluorescent nanoparticles for functionalized applications in detection and adsorption of cobalt (II)

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Facile fabrication of water-soluble fluorescent polymer nanoprobes for trace detection of cobalt ion (Co2+) is still a demand in environmental and biological fields. The sphere-like nanoscale PEI-MA was successfully synthesized by hyperbranched polyethyleneimine (hPEI) and mercaptosuccinic acid (MA) via hydrothermal treatment. The synthesized PEI-MA had good selectivity and sensitivity, with a wide detection range (0–200 μM) and a low detection limit (0.27 μM) to Co2+ in aqueous solution. The low bio-toxicity and cell permeability were demonstrated by HeLa cell imaging, indicating the PEI-MA with certain potential could be utilized in the field of biosensing domain. Meanwhile, the PEI-MA was employed in adjustable fluorescent dyes and fabrication of portable solid sensors. Among these PEI-DA-doped sensors, test strips and nanofibrous fluorescent films with remarkable fluorescent performance could be used as effective monitoring devices for the detection of Co2+. Moreover, the dual-functional fluorescent hydrogels owned functions of detection and adsorption for Co2+ in water system, providing ingenious reference for the interdisciplinary research between fluorescent sensing and adsorption of Co2+.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent probe-based assay is currently one of pivotal and convenient methods in environmental and biosensing fields [1–3], and fluorescent probes are broadly divided into traditional conjugate molecules [4,5] and emerging polymer nanoprobes [6,7]. The conjugate probes provide good quantum yields [8], specific design routes [9], well-defined luminescence mechanisms [10], and various colors of fluorescence [11]. However, utilization of organic solvent, poor water solubility and high cytotoxicity for conjugate molecules are several fatal issues that have to be considered [12–15].

Many polymer nanoprobes, which could overcome the problems induced by conjugated structures, have been developed based on eco-friendly polymer matrix [16,17]. Generally speaking, the emerging polymer nanoprobes own good water-solubility and compatibility; the synthetic process of them is concise; the raw materials of synthesizing them are economic [18,19]. Meanwhile, polymer-based probes have been widely used in the detection of antibiotics [20], metal ions [21], tetracyclines [22,23], etc. In addition, the excellent compatibility makes them more suitable for producing portable sensors through functionalized designs. Therefore, developing novel polymer probes and its functional applications are significant and challenging.

Cobalt ion (Co2+), a trace element in living organisms [24,25], is an essential component of vitamin B12 [26,27]. However, excessive accumulation of Co2+ may cause toxicological effects and diseases such as ductal diastole and cardiomyopathy [28]. In addition, as an essential raw material for cobalt salts, cobalt has a widespread application in the hard metal industry as well [29]. Up to the current time, many analysis methods and fluorescent probes have been developed for detecting trace cobalt ions (Co2+), and these methods include Atomic Absorption Spectrophotometry, Inductively Coupled Plasma Mass Spectroscopy, Auger Electron Spectroscopy, absorption, and chemiluminescence [30–32]. Compared with these traditional technologies, fluorescent probe-based technologies show obvious advantages in efficiently detecting Co2+. In recent years, conjugated molecular probes [15], nitrogen-sulfur doped graphene quantum dots [33], boron-nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots [1], nitrogen-rich carbon quantum dots [34], and silicon nanoparticles [35] have been reported to detect Co2+. Among these sensors, there are rare portable functional sensors for Co2+ in adsorption and detection, thus the development and design of Co2+ solid-phase sensors are in great demand.

From the respective of this paper, nanoscale polymer dots named PEI-MA, with strong blue fluorescence, could be applied for trace detection of Co2+ by hyperbranched polyethyleneimine (hPEI) and mercaptosuccinic acid (MA) via hydrothermal reaction. The synthesized PEI-MA possessed tiny average dimension, excellent water solubility and optical properties while exhibited the low biotoxicity and cell permeability for living cells. Through further design and development, the PEI-MA was employed in fabricating functional sensors which included test strips, nanofibrous films and dual-functional hydrogels. The test strips and nanofibrous films were effectively able to respond to Co2+, and the PEI-MA-doped fluorescent hydrogels had capabilities of monitoring and removal for Co2+ in aqueous solution.

Synthesizing by hydrothermal reaction (180 °C, 8 h) and self-assembly as illustrated in Scheme 1 , the novel PEI-MA could be successfully employed in the detection of Co2+. Meanwhile, the microscopic appearance and chemical structure of PEI-MA were determined by transmission electron microscope (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern, fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Firstly, the TEM demonstrated that PEI-MA as nanoscale sphere-like particles possesses good dispersion, with an average particle size measured to be 10.72 nm (Fig. 1 a). The broad peak at a 2θ of about 23.9° in the XRD pattern indicated that PEI-MA was an amorphous carbon structure [36–38] (Fig. 1b). In the FT-IR spectroscopy, a new distinct peak located at 1672 cm−1 was attributed to the formation of amide linkage (–CONH–), which confirmed the PEI-MA was synthesized by the amidation reaction between carboxylic acids and amine groups [39] (Fig. 1c). By comparison with 1H NMR of hPEI and MA (Fig. S1a), the PEI-MA showed a new peak at 8.31 ppm could be attributed to the existence of amide hydrogen (Fig. 1d), whose result was consistent with the scope of occurrence of amide bonds [40,41].

As a further analysis of PEI-MA, elements composition and chemical bonds were further characterized by XPS spectra. In the full XPS pattern of PEI-MA (Fig. 1e and Fig. S1b), the new peaks of O 1s and S 2p were observed at 532 and 164 eV compared with hPEI respectively [42], which demonstrated the successful conjunction of MA and hPEI. The peak at 531.4 eV in the high-resolution O 1s spectrum was attributed to the CO bond (Fig. 1f), indicating the existence of numerous amide groups in PEI-MA [43]. For the S 2p spectrum (Fig. 1g), it could be disintegrated into two peaks at 162.20 and 163.47 eV, which was corresponding to S 2p1/2 and S 2p2/3 of sulfydryl [44] respectively. That the high-resolution C 1s spectrum presented the peaks at 284.49, 285.07, 285.79, and 288.08 eV were ascribed to C–C, C–N, C–S and CO bonds [45] (Fig. 1h). Meanwhile, the amine and amide bonds at 397.84 and 398.71 eV were co-existing in the high-resolution N 1s spectrum [46] (Fig. 1i). All the above characterizations confirmed that a novel sulfur-oxygen-doped polymer nanoprobe had been successfully synthesized.

The spectroscopic properties of PEI-MA were verified by UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra. In Fig. 2 a, the two precursors of hPEI and MA were transparent and no peak after 250 nm in aqueous solution. However, yellow-colored PEI-MA generated a new absorption peak at 327 nm, which indicated the n→π* transition of oxygen-atom-containing double bonds (–CONH–) [47]. In the excitation-emission spectrum (Fig. 2b), the PEI-MA had strong blue fluorescence under the ultraviolet light (UV), with excitation and emission peaks at 354 and 474 nm, respectively. That the PEI-MA's spectra exhibited a single peak and a large Stokes shift (120 nm) allowed to significantly eliminate the interference between excitation and emission. The emission peak of PEI-MA redshifted slightly with increasing excitation wavelength (340–380 nm) in Fig. 2c, and the excitation-dependent-emission phenomenon was attributed to irregular surface and variable size distribution.

The PEI-MA exhibited bright blue fluorescence, and the Commission International De L'Eclairage (CIE) coordinates of PEI-MA at (0.17, 0.24) which was calculated from fluorescent emission curves (Fig. 2d). During fluorescent titration experiments (Fig. 2e and f), the emission of PEI-MA revealed a regular decreasing trend with the incremental concentration of Co2+, which indicated there was a good linear correlation of (F
0
–F)/F
0 versus the concentration of Co2+ (R2 = 0.9986). At the same time, the detection limit of PEI-MA to Co2+ was calculated to be 0.27 μM, which was dramatically lower than that (1.7 × 10−5 M) recommended in drinking water by environment protection agency (EPA) [48]. Meanwhile, the fluorescent solution of PEI-MA was quenched observably within 40 s after the addition of Co2+ (Fig. S3a), which demonstrated the PEI-MA had a rapid response time. During competition experiments, that only Co2+ could effectively quench the fluorescence (Fig. 2g) indicated that PEI-MA owned good selectivity and sensitivity. In addition, the PEI-MA owned good storage stability that could be stored in aqueous solution at least half a year at room temperature (Fig. S3b).

The PEI-MA maintained good fluorescence performance in different pH conditions (Fig. 2h), indicating the PEI-MA could be adapted to most natural environmental conditions. The fluorescence emission intensity of PEI-MA reached its maximum and minimum values when pH was at 5 and 1, respectively. That the fluorescence intensity declined in strongly acidic solution was caused by the protonation effect [49]. In addition, the salt-tolerance experiments showed that the fluorescence of PEI-MA could still be maintained over 92% in 500 mM saline solution (Fig. 2i). These results suggested that PEI-MA with good anti-interference and immense potential could be applied to complex detection conditions.

The fluorescence intensity of PEI-MA suffered a plunge after the addition of Co2+, and the quenching mechanism was discussed and analyzed as follow. In the UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. 3 a), as Co2+ combined with PEI-MA in aqueous solution, the color of PEI-MA solution became darker. Meanwhile, the absorption peak at 327 nm was blue-shifted to 314 nm, which was attributed to the occurrence of complexation effect [50]. In addition, the changes of amine and amide groups were observed in the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 3b), illustrating that Co2+ processes the capability to affect electron-rich groups in PEI-MA. In the XPS characterizations of PEI-MA + Co2+ complex (Fig. 3c–g), the characteristic peak of Co 2p was found to be stable [29]. The peaks at 796.14 and 779.66 eV in high-resolution Co 2p spectrum were assigned to be the spin-orbit splitting of Co 2p1/2 and Co 2p2/3 51, illustrating the existence of Co2+ in the complex. Moreover, the slight shifts of the S/N/O-containing groups were observed under the impact of Co2+, whose results were consistent with the FT-IR and UV–Vis spectrum.

Zeta potential analysis revealed that the surface charges of PEI-MA and PEI-MA + Co2+ complex were measured to +8.71 mV and +13.50 mV (Fig. 3h), respectively. The increasing of zeta potential demonstrated that the Co2+ successfully combined with PEI-MA and generated more stable weakly-fluorescent complex [51]. Moreover, the fluorescence lifetimes of PEI-MA and PEI-MA + Co2+ were calculated to be 4.73 ns and 4.65 ns in Fig. 3i, respectively. The slight change in the fluorescence lifetime and other conclusions confirmed that the quenching mechanism triggered by Co2+ was static quenching [52].

The excessive existence of Co2+ in the organism might lead to several physiological diseases, thus providing biological assays of PEI-MA were particularly essential. The MTT assays of PEI-MA provided satisfactory cell viability of HeLa cells cultured in different concentrations of PEI-MA stock solution (Fig. S4), which demonstrated the PEI-MA possessed low cytotoxicity and ideal biocompatibility. In the cell-based bioluminescence imaging (Fig. 4 ), the HeLa cells were first cultured with PEI-MA stock solution and exhibited bright fluorescence in blue channel, while the Co2+ quenched sharply that intense fluorescence. In addition, the outstanding permeability, which was verified through overlay of blue channel images and bright field transmission images, demonstrated the PEI-MA with promising prospect could be utilized to image exogenous Co2+ in living cells.

The functionalization of fluorescent probes was very attractive and challenging, providing convenience and efficiency for real-time detection [53,54]. However, there were rigorous requirements for fluorescent probes themselves, such as bright fluorescence, facile manufacture, good compatibility and dispersibility. Since the PEI-MA displayed intense fluorescence in both aggregation and dispersion, the nanoparticles had been employed directly in the fabrications of fluorescent dyes and portable sensors. In Fig. 5 a, that the solvent-free PEI-MA were drawn in the logo of “the 14th National Games of the People's Republic of China” illustrated it could be utilized in the anti-counterfeit fields. Moreover, the fluorescent aggregation could be dramatically darkened after the addition of quencher, which indicated Co2+ could serve as a regulator of the intensity of fluorescence dyes.

In Fig. 5b, the PEI-MA-doped test strips with high designability exhibited strong fluorescence, while the fluorescence was quenched significantly after immersion of Co2+ solution (100 μM). Meanwhile, the paper-based sensors quenched differently according to various concentration of Co2+, indicating test strips had capability of qualitative and quantitative detection. Through electrospinning technique, the PEI-MA and polylactic acid were fabricated as sustainable nanofibrous films which with intense fluorescence could selectively and rapidly monitor trace Co2+ (Fig. 5c). Meanwhile, that PEI-MA-doped films had a tiny nanometer diameter and a larger specific surface area, which revealed by the SEM image (Fig. 5d), were conductive to immediately respond with quencher.

The detection and removal of Co2+ in aqueous solution was very significant for environmental protection, while there were few Co2+ sensors that could coexist with adsorption and sensing functions. Under such circumstances, PEI-MA-doped dual-functional hydrogels were facilely fabricated via photo-initiated polymerization, using acrylic-acrylamide hydrogel as a platform (Fig. 6 a). In Fig. 6b, the pure acrylic-acrylamide hydrogel was transparent and non-fluorescent, while the PEI-MA-doped hydrogel displayed strong fluorescence. After immersion in Co2+ solution, that the fluorescence intensity darked sharply indicated this sensing hydrogel could be an effective tool for the detection of Co2+. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of fluorescent hydrogels decreased obviously with increased concentration of Co2+ (Fig. 6c), which indicated PEI-MA-doped hydrogel had enormous potentialities for quantitative detection of Co2+ in water system.

The cellular microstructure and a loose reticulation of dual-functional hydrogel in the solvent-free condition was observed in Fig. 6d, facilitating the processes of ion adsorption and detection. Furthermore, the adsorption properties of sensing hydrogel on Co2+ revealed that the fitted variance of the Langmuir isothermal adsorption model exceeded Freundlich's fit (Fig. 6e), which suggested the adsorption model on the surface of the adsorbent material was homogeneous and belongs to monomolecular layer adsorption [55]. Simultaneously, the adsorption kinetic data exhibited that the adsorption growth rate increased slowly to reach the equilibrium after 200 min, and the adsorption capacity was about 14.7 mg/g (Fig. 6f). The linear correlation coefficients fitted with the secondary kinetic model were better than the primary kinetic results, inferring that the adsorption process was chemisorption of the adsorbent-adsorbent interaction [56]. Hence, the preparation of PEI-MA-doped hydrogels could be a considerably reference for the intersection of the of metal-ion sensing and adsorption.

The novel nanoscale PEI-MA with green and facile synthetic process exhibited blue fluorescence could be employed in the detection of Co2+ was successfully synthesized via one-opt reaction. Exhibiting good selectivity and sensitivity towards Co2+, owning the merits of low cytotoxicity and eco-friendly detection, the PEI-MA demonstrated promising prospect in the fields of biosensing and environmental detection. Equipping with eco-friendly, sustainable and low-cost fabrication, PEI-MA-doped test strips, nanofibrous films and dual-functional hydrogels serve as Co2+ sensors which exhibited designability and practicability in the functional applications. Moreover, the fluorescent hydrogel was also capable of removing and detecting Co2+, which was highly innovative for the integration of the relevant researches between fluorescent detection and metal-ion adsorption.

Jin Yang: Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Weixing Chen: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Yiting Ma: Data curation, Investigation. Geoffrey Bright: Writing – review & editing. Xile Jie: Methodology, Investigation. Huaqi Zhao: Methodology, Data curation. Hongwei Zhou: Methodology, Formal analysis. Xilang Jin: Supervision, Validation, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have influenced the work reported in this study.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21807085).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

Synthesis procedure, experimental, spectroscopic properties of PEI-MA, detection limit of PEI-MA, MTT assay and other supporting materials (PDF). The supporting Information is available free of charge at http://pubs.acs.org.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2022.110271.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of B,N-doped CQDs as versatile fluorescence probes for sensitive detection of cobalt ions in environmental water and biological samples
Microchem J

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent spherical sponge cellulose sensors for highly selective and semiquantitative visual analysis: detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ions
